old_claim.note: Crystal Growth Techniques provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from the broad semiconductors node to crystal_growth_techniques; high-purity crystal growth belongs to later semiconductor manufacturing, not the initial material/rectifier effect.

source_shape.source_locator: Milestone text describing the 1874 point contact between a metal wire and a galena crystal.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The CHM milestone describes an early semiconductor rectifier using a naturally occurring galena crystal contact, not high-purity engineered crystal growth.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This rejects crystal growth as a prerequisite for the broad node because engineered crystal production is later manufacturing capability, while the scoped node includes early mineral-crystal rectification.

ontology_before: The broad Semiconductors node mixed early semiconductor behavior with later high-purity semiconductor manufacturing inputs.
ontology_after: The Semiconductors node covers materials and early rectifier behavior; crystal growth remains available for later fabrication and device-manufacturing nodes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: later crystal-growth manufacturing capability no longer reaches every semiconductor dependent through the broad semiconductors node.
would_reject_if: The semiconductors node were narrowed to mean high-purity single-crystal semiconductor wafers only.
A source showed that early semiconductor rectifier effects required engineered crystal growth rather than existing mineral crystals.
Removing the edge also removed crystal-growth context from narrower wafer or device-fabrication nodes where it remains appropriate.
short_reason: High-purity crystal growth is manufacturing context for later devices, not a prerequisite for the broad 1874 semiconductor rectifier effect.
